title: Plastic waste generation
subtitle: This measures total plastic waste generation prior to management and therefore does not represent the quantity of plastic at risk of polluting waterways, rivers and the ocean environment.
caption: The choropleth map displays total plastic waste generation for the year 2010, using a continuous color scale ranging from light beige for zero tonnes to deep maroon for 10 million tonnes. The visualization reveals a highly concentrated distribution of waste generation, with the highest values clustered in specific industrialized nations and major emerging economies. The United States and China appear in the darkest maroon shade, placing them in the 10 million tonne band, representing the global maximums. Brazil also falls within this highest category, distinguished as the only South American nation in the top bracket. Moving down the scale, Russia and Germany are visible in a dark orange-red band corresponding to the 7.5 to 10 million tonne range. A broad middle tier of waste generation, falling between 2.5 and 5 million tonnes, includes countries such as India, Mexico, and Japan. Western European nations like the United Kingdom, France, and Italy occupy the 5 to 7.5 million tonne band, while Canada and Australia sit in the 1 to 2.5 million tonne range. In contrast, the vast majority of the African continent, along with Greenland and parts of Central Asia, appear in the lightest beige, indicating generation levels between 0 and 1 million tonnes. Several regions, including parts of Africa and the Middle East, are marked with diagonal hatching to denote missing data. The geographic pattern shows that the largest volumes of plastic waste in 2010 were generated by a small number of populous and economically active countries, while most of the world's landmass contributed significantly lower amounts.
